name: Electronic Blocks
short_description: Stackable electronic LEGO Duplo blocks that let preschoolers build the sense-think-act loop by hand, programming behaviour with no keyboard, mouse, or screen.
long_description: Electronic Blocks are tangible programming elements created by Peta Wyeth and Gordon Wyeth, presented at INTERACT 2001. Built on LEGO Duplo bricks, they come in three kinds: sensor blocks (light, sound, touch), action blocks (lights, sound, movement), and logic blocks (and, not, toggle) that pass signals upward through the stack. By snapping blocks together, children aged roughly 3-8 assemble working behaviours such as a light that flashes when you clap or a robot that reacts to touch, making the sense-think-act loop physically graspable. The project matters as an early demonstration that the core loop of an embodied, reactive system can be expressed as a tangible interaction rather than abstract code, prefiguring later tangible-programming and embodied-computing toolkits.
ai_note: The blocks are fixed-function sensing, signal-passing logic, and actuation hardware; there is no machine learning or adaptive model running in the system.

images.caption: Figure from Wyeth & Wyeth (2001) showing stacked sensor, logic, and action Electronic Blocks built on LEGO Duplo bricks.
images.alt_text: Chunky plastic blocks resembling LEGO Duplo bricks stacked into a tower, each block embedded with electronics for sensing, logic, or action.

sources.title: Electronic Blocks: Tangible Programming Elements for Preschoolers
sources.publisher: INTERACT 2001 (IFIP TC13 Conference on Human-Computer Interaction)

sources.title: Electronic blocks: Tangible programming elements for preschoolers (full paper)
How Young Children Learn to Program With Sensor, Action, and Logic Blocks

interactions.title: Snap a touch block onto a light
interactions.description: A child stacks a touch sensor block directly under a light action block; press the sensor plate and the bulb lights up, let go and it goes dark. The simplest stack makes the sense-then-act link physical in one move.

interactions.title: A lamp that hates noise
interactions.description: Slide a NOT logic block between a hearing block and a light block, and the light glows only when the room is quiet and switches off the moment someone makes a sound, letting a preschooler build and feel a logical inversion by hand.

interactions.title: A light that flashes when you clap
interactions.description: Pair a hearing sensor block with a light block so the bulb blinks each time a child claps, turning a noisy room into the program's input and the child's own body into the trigger.

interactions.title: Drive a four-wheeled block-car by sound
interactions.description: The movement action block is a small four-wheel car; stacking a sensor on top lets children steer it with claps or touch, building improvised remote-control cars and robots out of the same stackable bricks.

interactions.title: Robots that crash into each other
interactions.description: Children aged four to six built moving block-robots that respond to light or sound and sent them careening into one another, discovering reactive behaviour through play rather than instruction.

interactions.title: Six days in a real preschool
interactions.description: Across a six-day evaluation, more than thirty preschool children assembled and debugged working block stacks with minimal adult help, showing the sense-think-act loop could be taught without screen, keyboard, or symbolic code.
interactions.source_label: Wyeth, Journal of the Learning Sciences (2008)

interactions.title: A delayed-reaction night light
interactions.description: Using the delay logic block between a sensor and a light, a child could build a lamp that stays on for a beat after a clap or touch before fading, exploring the idea of timed behaviour with the same physical grammar of stacking.

interactions.title: A burglar-alarm tower for the play corner
interactions.description: Stack a hearing block, a toggle logic block, and a sound block so the tower latches into an alarm the first time it hears a noise and keeps wailing until reset, giving children a graspable model of state and memory.
